era_context: Early Iron Age II in the Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The kingdoms of Israel and Judah are established as separate political entities. Egypt's 22nd Dynasty (Libyan) is at the height of its power under Osorkon I. This year may mark the date of Shoshenq I's major military campaign into the Levant, one of the most significant events of the early Iron Age II period, though the dating remains disputed among scholars.

events.title: Shoshenq I (Shishak) campaigns into Levant; invasion of Israel and Judah
events.region: Egypt, Kingdom of Israel, Kingdom of Judah, Levant
events.description: Pharaoh Shoshenq I (biblical Shishak) launches a major military campaign into the Levant, invading both the Kingdom of Israel and the Kingdom of Judah. According to the biblical account, Shoshenq captures numerous cities and extracts tribute from Rehoboam of Judah. The Karnak temple reliefs of Shoshenq I list conquered cities, providing Egyptian corroboration of the campaign. The campaign represents a major assertion of Egyptian power in the Levant and a significant military and economic shock to the region. The campaign may have weakened both Israel and Judah, affecting the balance of power in the region for decades. However, the exact date of the campaign is disputed; some scholars place it earlier (c. 930 BCE) or later (c. 920 BCE).

sources.name: Karnak temple reliefs and inscriptions of Shoshenq I

events.certainty_note: The biblical account places Shishak's invasion in the fifth year of Rehoboam's reign. If Rehoboam began c. 930 BCE, this would place the invasion c. 925 BCE. However, chronological uncertainty of ±5–10 years is common for this period. The Egyptian evidence (Karnak reliefs) confirms a campaign by Shoshenq I into the Levant but does not provide a precise date. Some scholars argue the campaign occurred c. 920 BCE or even earlier (c. 945 BCE in high chronology models).
cross_references: Contemporary with -925-002 (Rehoboam's reign) and -925-003 (Jeroboam I's reign)
Part of broader pattern of Egyptian-Levantine interaction

events.title: Rehoboam of Judah pays tribute to Shoshenq I; Judah weakened
events.description: According to the biblical account, Rehoboam of Judah is forced to pay tribute to Shoshenq I following the Egyptian invasion. The biblical text states that Shoshenq takes the treasures of the temple and the royal palace. This event represents a significant loss of wealth and prestige for Judah and may have weakened the kingdom's ability to defend itself against future threats. The kingdom's economy would have been strained by the tribute payments.
events.certainty_note: The biblical account is the primary source for this event. The dating depends on the dating of Shoshenq I's campaign, which is disputed. The Karnak reliefs confirm the campaign but do not provide details about tribute payments.
cross_references: Affects subsequent political developments in Judah

events.title: Jeroboam I's reign continues; northern Kingdom of Israel affected by Egyptian campaign
events.description: Jeroboam I continues to rule the northern Kingdom of Israel. If Shoshenq I's campaign occurs in 925 BCE, Israel would also be affected by the Egyptian invasion, with cities captured and tribute extracted. The Karnak reliefs list both Judahite and Israelite cities, suggesting that Shoshenq's campaign affected both kingdoms. The campaign may have weakened Israel's military capacity and economic resources.

sources.name: Karnak temple reliefs and inscriptions of Shoshenq I

events.certainty_note: The biblical account focuses on Judah but mentions that Shoshenq also captured cities in Israel. The Karnak reliefs list cities from both kingdoms, confirming that Israel was also affected by the campaign.
cross_references: Contemporary with -925-002 (Rehoboam's tribute to Shoshenq I)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Shoshenq I's campaign is one of the most contentious issues in ancient Near Eastern chronology. The biblical text places it in the fifth year of Rehoboam's reign, but the absolute date depends on when Rehoboam's reign began, which is itself disputed. Scholarly estimates for the campaign range from 945 BCE (high chronology) to 920 BCE (low chronology), a span of 25 years. The Karnak reliefs list cities conquered by Shoshenq I, but the list includes both Judahite and Israelite cities, and some scholars question whether all listed cities were actually conquered or whether the list is partly propagandistic. Archaeological evidence for destruction layers corresponding to Shoshenq's campaign is inconsistent across sites, with some showing destruction in the 10th century and others in the 9th century. The biblical narrative may conflate or redate the campaign for theological purposes. Some scholars argue that the campaign was less extensive than the biblical and Egyptian sources suggest, or that it occurred at a different time entirely.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 925 BCE is dominated by the account of Shoshenq I's campaign, which is attested in both biblical and Egyptian sources. However, the dating of the campaign is highly disputed, with scholarly estimates differing by up to 25 years. The biblical text was compiled centuries after the events and reflects theological concerns. The Egyptian reliefs are contemporary but are royal propaganda and may not accurately represent the extent or nature of the campaign. Archaeological evidence from Levantine sites provides material context but does not independently confirm the specific date of the campaign. The chronological uncertainty for this period is substantial, and the placement of Shoshenq's campaign in 925 BCE is a conventional choice based on one interpretation of the biblical chronology. Other scholars would place the campaign in different years. The record is biased toward major military and political events; economic, social, and cultural developments are poorly documented.

graph_edges.note: Shoshenq I's campaign leads to Rehoboam's tribute payment
Shoshenq I's campaign affects both Israel and Judah simultaneously
The weakening of Judah by Shoshenq's campaign may have affected subsequent political developments
